perturbation.original_text: The obligation not to disclose Confidential Information shall remain in effect until 2 years from the date hereof or until the Confidential Information ceases to be a trade secret, except to the extent that such Confidential Information is excluded from the obligations of confidentiality under this Agreement pursuant to Paragraph 2 above.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligation not to disclose Confidential Information shall remain in effect until 1 year from the date hereof or until the Confidential Information ceases to be a trade secret, except to the extent that such Confidential Information is excluded from the obligations of confidentiality under this Agreement pursuant to Paragraph 2 above.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Minnesota law, which often requires a longer period depending on the industry and nature of the information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota's Uniform Trade Secrets Act dictates what constitutes a trade secret and provides legal remedies for misappropriation. A one-year confidentiality period may be deemed unreasonably short to protect certain trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the Act's intent to provide adequate protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.01 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.01 325C.02 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.01 Version List 325C.01 DEFINITIONS. § Subdivision 1. Scope. As used in sections 325C.01 to 325C.07 , the terms defined in these sections have the meanings given them, unless the context requires otherwise. § Subd. 2. Improper means. "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. § Subd. 3. Misappropriation. "Misappropriation" means: (i) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the discloser's or user's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of the discloser's or user's position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. § Subd. 4. Person.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract reduces the confidentiality period from 2 years to 1 year. The Perturbation Explanation states that this reduction may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Minnesota law. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that a one-year period may be deemed unreasonably short and conflict with the intent of Minnesota's Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' which involves the disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent, implying a need for adequate protection, which the shortened period may undermine. While the snippet doesn't explicitly state a required duration, the potential for the shortened period to fail in adequately protecting trade secrets creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All notices given under this Agreement must be in writing. A notice is effective upon receipt and shall be sent via one of the following methods: delivery in person, overnight courier service, certified or registered mail, postage prepaid, return receipt requested, addressed to the party to be notified at the below address or by facsimile at the below facsimile number or in the case of either party, to such other party, address or facsimile number as such party may designate upon reasonable notice to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: All notices given under this Agreement must be in writing. A notice is effective upon sending and shall be sent via one of the following methods: delivery in person, overnight courier service, certified or registered mail, postage prepaid, return receipt requested, addressed to the party to be notified at the below address or by facsimile at the below facsimile number or in the case of either party, to such other party, address or facsimile number as such party may designate upon reasonable notice to the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the effective time of a notice from receipt to sending could create issues regarding proof of service, especially if the notice is never actually received. Minnesota law requires proof of receipt in certain situations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 5.02
perturbation.law_explanation: The Minnesota Rules of Civil Procedure often require proof of service, implying actual receipt, for certain legal documents. Stipulating that notice is effective upon sending may not satisfy these requirements, especially in situations where legal deadlines are triggered by receipt of notice.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 502. Attorney-Client Privilege and Work Product; Limitations on Waiver | Federal Rules of Evidence | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 502. Attorney-Client Privilege and Work Product; Limitations on Waiver The following provisions apply, in the circumstances set out, to disclosure of a communication or information covered by the attorney-client privilege or work-product protection. (a) Disclosure Made in a Federal Proceeding or to a Federal Office or Agency; Scope of a Waiver. When the disclosure is made in a federal proceeding or to a federal office or agency and waives the attorney-client privilege or work-product protection, the waiver extends to an undisclosed communication or information in a federal or state proceeding only if: (1) the waiver is intentional; (2) the disclosed and undisclosed communications or information concern the same subject matter; and (3) they ought in fairness to be considered together. (b) Inadvertent Disclosure. When made in a federal proceeding or to a federal office or agency, the disclosure does not operate as a waiver in a federal or state proceeding if: (1) the disclosure is inadvertent; (2) the holder of the privilege or protection took reasonable steps to prevent disclosure; and (3) the holder promptly took reasonable steps to rectify the error, including (if applicable) following Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 26 (b)(5)(B). (c) Disclosure Made in a State Proceeding. When the disclosure is made in a state proceeding and is not the subject of a state-court order concerning waiver, the disclosure does not operate as a waiver in a federal proceeding if the disclosure: (1) would not be a waiver under this rule if it had been made in a federal proceeding; or (2) is not a waiver under the law of the state where the disclosure occurred. (d) Controlling Effect of a Court Order. A federal court may order that the privilege or protection is not waived by disclosure connected with the litigation pending before the court — in which event the disclosure is also not a waiver in any other federal or state proceeding. (e) Controlling Effect of a Party Agreement. An agreement on the effect of disclosure in a federal proceeding is binding only on the parties to the agreement, unless it is incorporated into a court order. (f) Controlling Effect of this Rule. Notwithstanding Rules 101 and 1101 , this rule applies to state proceedings and to federal court-annexed and federal court-mandated arbitration proceedings, in the circumstances set out in the rule. And notwithstanding Rule 501, this rule applies even if state law provides the rule of decision. (g) Definitions. In this rule: (1) “attorney-client privilege” means the protection that applicable law provides for confidential attorney-client communications; and (2) “work-product protection” means the protection that applicable law provides for tangible material (or its intangible equivalent) prepared in anticipation of litigation or for trial. Notes (Added Pub. L. 110–322, §1(a), Sept. 19, 2008, 122 Stat. 3537; Apr. 26, 2011, eff. Dec. 1, 2011.) Explanatory Note on Evidence Rule 502 The following explanatory note was prepared by the Judicial Conference Advisory Committee on Evidence Rules, revised Nov. 28, 2007: This new rule has two major purposes: 1) It resolves some longstanding disputes in the courts about the effect of certain disclosures of communications or information protected by the attorney-client privilege or as work product—specifically those disputes involving inadvertent disclosure and subject matter waiver. 2) It responds to the widespread complaint that litigation costs necessary to protect against waiver of attorney-client privilege or work product have become prohibitive due to the concern that any disclosure (however innocent or minimal) will operate as a subject matter waiver of all protected communications or information. This concern is especially troubling in cases i
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the point at which a notice is considered effective, from receipt to sending. This alteration creates a potential conflict with legal requirements in Minnesota that necessitate proof of receipt for service of legal documents. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly address this situation, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both highlight the potential contradiction with Minnesota Rules of Civil Procedure, which may require proof of service, implying receipt. By making the notice effective upon sending, the changed text weakens the ability to provide proof of actual receipt, increasing the risk of non-compliance and legal challenges.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will terminate on the earlier of: (a) the written agreement of the parties to terminate this Agreement; (b) the consummation of the Transaction; or (c) 5 years from the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will terminate on the earlier of: (a) the written agreement of the parties to terminate this Agreement; (b) the consummation of the Transaction; or (c) 10 years from the date hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the termination period to 10 years may be considered an unreasonably long duration for a non-disclosure agreement under Minnesota law, especially if it unduly restricts an individual's ability to work or compete.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statute § 325N.01 governs covenants not to compete. While this agreement is primarily a non-disclosure agreement, a very long confidentiality period (10 years) could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint on trade if it effectively prevents a party from using general skills and knowledge acquired during the protected period.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325N.01 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325N Section 325N.01 325N.02 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325N Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325N.01 Version List Recent History 2021 325N.01 Amended 2021 c 7 s 5 2015 325N.01 Amended 2015 c 50 s 1 2015 325N.01 Amended 2015 c 44 s 32 2013 325N.01 Amended 2013 c 17 s 1 2009 325N.01 Amended 2009 c 141 s 1 2007 325N.01 Amended 2007 c 106 s 1 2007 325N.01 Amended 2007 c 57 art 3 s 43 2004 325N.01 New 2004 c 263 s 1 325N.01 DEFINITIONS. The definitions in paragraphs (a) to (h) apply to sections 325N.01 to 325N.09 . (a) "Solicitor" means any person who, directly or indirectly, makes any solicitation, representation, or offer to any owner to perform for compensation or who, for compensation, performs any service which the person in any manner represents will in any manner do any of the following: (1) stop or postpone a foreclosure sale, a tax forfeiture sale, or a contract for deed termination; (2) obtain any forbearance from any beneficiary, local unit of government, association of apartment owners, contract for deed seller, or mortgagee regarding: (i) taxes; (ii) dues owed to an association of apartment owners; or (iii) contract for deed, mortgage, or any other payments; (3) assist the owner to: (i) exercise the right of reinstatement provided in section 580.30 or the right of redemption provided in section 580.25 ; (ii) exercise the right of redemption under chapter 281 or the right to repurchase under sections 282.241 to 282.324 ; (iii) cure a default that has resulted in a termination notice issued under s
perturbation.justification: The modification extends the termination period of the agreement from 5 to 10 years. The Law Explanation suggests that under Minnesota Statute § 325N.01, a very long confidentiality period (like 10 years) could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint on trade if it prevents a party from using general skills and knowledge acquired during the protected period. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 primarily defines 'Solicitor' and foreclosure-related terms, it indicates that Minnesota Statute § 325N.01 exists and governs trade regulations and consumer protection, which the Law Explanation suggests applies to non-compete agreements. Thus, extending the confidentiality period to 10 years could contradict the implied limitations on non-compete agreements under Minnesota law, making the changed agreement potentially unenforceable. This falls under the 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' definition because the time-sensitive obligation (termination date) does not align with legal requirements regarding reasonable restraint of trade.

perturbation.original_text: Each party consents to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts located in the State of Minnesota for any legal action, suit or proceeding arising out of or in connection with this Agreement. Each party further waives any objection to the laying of venue for any such suit, action or proceeding in such courts.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party consents to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts located in the State of Delaware for any legal action, suit or proceeding arising out of or in connection with this Agreement. Each party further waives any objection to the laying of venue for any such suit, action or proceeding in such courts.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the jurisdiction to Delaware when the company is located and operates primarily in Minnesota might violate Minnesota Statute § 543.19, the long-arm statute, if the defendant has insufficient contacts with Delaware.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota's long-arm statute, § 543.19, requires sufficient minimum contacts with the state for its courts to exercise personal jurisdiction over a non-resident defendant. Selecting Delaware as the exclusive jurisdiction, when the company operates in Minnesota, may violate this statute if the defendant lacks sufficient connections with Delaware, making the forum selection clause potentially unenforceable in Minnesota.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 543.19 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes CIVIL PROCEDURE Chapter 543 Section 543.19 543.18 543.20 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 543 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 543.19 Version List Recent History 2008 Subd. 1 Amended 2008 c 185 s 1 543.19 PERSONAL JURISDICTION OVER NONRESIDENTS. § Subdivision 1. Personal jurisdiction. As to a cause of action arising from any acts enumerated in this subdivision, a court of this state with jurisdiction of the subject matter may exercise personal jurisdiction over any foreign corporation or any nonresident individual, or the individual's personal representative, in the same manner as if it were a domestic corporation or the individual were a resident of this state. This section applies if, in person or through an agent, the foreign corporation or nonresident individual: (1) owns, uses, or possesses any real or personal property situated in this state; or (2) transacts any business within the state; or (3) commits any act in Minnesota causing injury or property damage; or (4) commits any act outside Minnesota causing injury or property damage in Minnesota, subject to the following exceptions when no jurisdiction shall be found: (i) Minnesota has no substantial interest in providing a forum; or (ii) the burden placed on the defendant by being brought under the state's jurisdiction would violate fairness and substantial justice. [See Note.] § Subd. 2. Service of process. The service of process on any person who is subject to the jurisdiction of the courts of this state, as provided in this section, may be made by personally serving the summons upon the defendant
perturbation.justification: The change from Minnesota to Delaware as the exclusive jurisdiction may contradict Minnesota's long-arm statute § 543.19. The law snippet states that a Minnesota court can exercise personal jurisdiction over a non-resident if they transact business within the state. By changing the jurisdiction to Delaware, the contract attempts to bypass this statute, particularly if the defendant's connection to Delaware is minimal, making the forum selection clause potentially unenforceable in Minnesota.
